findings.title: Comment says L3 approval but code is a no-op pass
findings.description: The comment states that sensitive tools in the restricted zone 'require L3 approval via autonomy check below', implying the code delegates enforcement to a later check. However, the autonomy boundary check below only fires if the tool_name appears in _TOOL_AUTONOMY_MAP. If a tool is in _SENSITIVE_TOOLS but not in _TOOL_AUTONOMY_MAP, neither the security zone nor the autonomy check will block it. The comment creates a false sense of security by implying enforcement that may not exist for all sensitive tools.
findings.suggestion: Either verify that every tool in _SENSITIVE_TOOLS also has an entry in _TOOL_AUTONOMY_MAP, or add explicit L3 enforcement inline: `return await _request_human_approval(agent_id, tool_name, ...)`.

findings.title: Repeated 'Suppressed: %s' debug messages are ungreppable
findings.description: At least 15 exception handlers across websocket.py and agent_tools.py use the identical message 'Suppressed: %s'. When diagnosing production issues, searching logs for 'Suppressed' gives no indication of which code path failed. These replace the former `pass` statements, which is an improvement, but the message provides near-zero diagnostic value since every suppressed exception looks identical.
findings.suggestion: Give each log site a unique, descriptive prefix, e.g. `logger.debug('chat_history json parse failed: %s', e)`, `logger.debug('quota increment skipped: %s', e)`. This makes log grep immediately useful.

findings.title: Inline comments on audit blocks restate the obvious
findings.code_snippet:
# Audit: openclaw agent created
try:
    from app.core.policy import write_audit_event
    await write_audit_event(db, event_type="agent.created", ...)
findings.description: The comments '# Audit: openclaw agent created', '# Audit: agent created', '# Audit: agent updated', '# Audit: agent deleted', '# Audit: agent.started', '# Audit: agent.stopped' appear before every audit block. The `write_audit_event` call with its `event_type` parameter already communicates this. These comments will rot if the event_type string is changed without updating the comment, and they add no explanatory value beyond what the code states.
findings.suggestion: Remove these per-block audit comments. If the audit pattern needs explanation, add a single module-level docstring or a comment at the first occurrence explaining the audit pattern, not on every instance.

findings.title: Section header comments removed without replacement
findings.code_snippet:
async def _discover_resources(arguments: dict) -> str:
    """Search Smithery registry for MCP servers."""
findings.description: Three section header comments were removed in this diff: '# --- Resource Discovery Executors ---', '# --- Email Tool Helpers ---', and '# -- Fallback: try User table --'. In a 4660-line file, these section dividers serve as navigation landmarks. Removing them without adding an alternative (e.g., moving functions to separate modules) makes navigation harder in this very large file.
findings.suggestion: Either restore the section headers (they have high ROI in a 4600+ line file) or, preferably, extract these function groups into separate modules (e.g., agent_tools_mcp.py, agent_tools_email.py) which is the real fix for a file this size.

findings.title: call_llm_via_engine docstring lacks parameter documentation
findings.code_snippet: """Call LLM via the middleware-based execution engine."""
findings.description: The function `call_llm_via_engine` is a new 100-line function with 10 parameters (including callbacks). Its single-line docstring 'Call LLM via the middleware-based execution engine' gives no information about the callback contracts, return value semantics, or the middleware stack behavior. The sibling function `call_llm` has proper parameter documentation. As a public function called from `call_llm`, future maintainers need to understand the interface contract.
findings.suggestion: Add parameter documentation matching the style of the existing `call_llm` docstring, covering at minimum: what `on_chunk`, `on_tool_call`, `on_thinking` callbacks receive, what the return string contains, and when `[Engine returned empty content]` is returned.

findings.title: Removed comment 'Build rich prompt' was the only hint of why
findings.code_snippet:
from app.services.agent_context import build_agent_context
# Look up current user's display name so the agent knows who it's talking to
findings.description: The diff removed the comment '# Build rich prompt with soul, memory, skills, relationships' which explained *why* `build_agent_context` is called and what it aggregates. The function name alone does not convey that it pulls from soul.md, memory.md, skills/, and relationships. The remaining comment 'Look up current user's display name so the agent knows who it's talking to' is good but covers only one sub-step.
findings.suggestion: Restore or rephrase the high-level comment, e.g. '# Build system prompt from agent soul, memory, skills, and relationships'.

findings.title: Redundant module-level logger shadowed by local import
findings.description: The function `call_llm_via_engine` creates a local `import logging` and `_logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` on lines 113-114, despite the module already having `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` at module scope (line 20). This creates two loggers with the same name. The local `_logger` variable shadows the module `logger` within this function, adding confusion. The function's internal debug calls use `_logger.debug(...)` while all other functions in the module use `logger.debug(...)`.
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant `import logging` and `_logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` from inside the function. Use the module-level `logger` like every other function in this module.

positive_observations: Systematic replacement of bare `except: pass` with `except Exception as e: logger.debug/warning(...)` across all files is a significant improvement for debuggability.
The collaboration.py docstring was updated to accurately reflect the new Redis Streams implementation with file-based fallback, keeping documentation in sync with behavior.
Good use of structured logging with contextual parameters (agent_id, table name) in the new warning/debug messages in agents.py delete flow.
